title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
Most introductions to time series forecasting train a model to predict a single step ahead and stop there. But real applications almost never want one value. An energy operator needs the next 24 hours of load, a retailer needs the next six weeks of demand, a clinical model needs the next several days of bed occupancy. The moment the horizon \(H\) exceeds 1, a question appears that the one-step setup never forced you to answer: how do you produce \(H\) values from a model that, fundamentally, learns a mapping from past to future?
This is not a hyperparameter detail. The strategy you pick changes which loss the model actually minimizes, how forecast errors accumulate across the horizon, how many models you have to train and store, and whether the dependency structure between \(\hat{y}_{N+1}\) and \(\hat{y}_{N+5}\) is preserved or thrown away. Three strategies dominate practice: recursive, direct, and joint (the latter usually called Multiple-Output or MIMO in the literature). They embody genuinely different assumptions, and the empirical record on which is best is famously inconsistent — Ben Taieb et al. (2012) found contradictory conclusions across the prior literature, with some studies favoring recursive, others direct, others multi-output.
Throughout, a multi-step task means predicting the next \(H\) values \([y_{N+1}, \dots, y_{N+H}]\) of a series \([y_1, \dots, y_N]\), with \(H > 1\) the forecast horizon and \(d\) the number of past lags used as input (the embedding dimension).

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
Most introductions to time series forecasting train a model to predict a single step ahead and stop there. But real applications almost never want one value. An energy operator needs the next 24 hours of load, a retailer needs the next six weeks of demand, a clinical model needs the next several days of bed occupancy. The moment the horizon \(H\) exceeds 1, a question appears that the one-step setup never forced you to answer: how do you produce \(H\) values from a model that, fundamentally, learns a mapping from past to future?
This is not a hyperparameter detail. The strategy you pick changes which loss the model actually minimizes, how forecast errors accumulate across the horizon, how many models you have to train and store, and whether the dependency structure between \(\hat{y}_{N+1}\) and \(\hat{y}_{N+5}\) is preserved or thrown away. Three strategies dominate practice: recursive, direct, and joint (the latter usually called Multiple-Output or MIMO in the literature). They embody genuinely different assumptions, and the empirical record on which is best is famously inconsistent — Ben Taieb et al. (2012) found contradictory conclusions across the prior literature, with some studies favoring recursive, others direct, others multi-output.
Throughout, a multi-step task means predicting the next \(H\) values \([y_{N+1}, \dots, y_{N+H}]\) of a series \([y_1, \dots, y_N]\), with \(H > 1\) the forecast horizon and \(d\) the number of past lags used as input (the embedding dimension).

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
section: 2. Recursive: one model, fed its own output
text:
2. Recursive: one model, fed its own output
The recursive (also iterated) strategy is the oldest and most intuitive. You train a single one-step model \(f\):
\[
y_{t+1} = f(y_t, y_{t-1}, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) + w_t,
\tag{1}\]
where \(y_t \in \mathbb{R}\) is the value at time \(t\), the input is the vector of the \(d\) most recent lags \((y_t, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}\), \(w_t\) is the noise term, and \(f: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}\) is the learned mapping. The model is trained only on the one-step objective.
To forecast \(H\) steps, you apply \(\hat{f}\) repeatedly, feeding each prediction back in as if it were an observation:
\[
\hat{y}_{N+h} =
\begin{cases}
\hat{f}(y_N, \dots, y_{N-d+1}) & h = 1 \\[4pt]
\hat{f}(\hat{y}_{N+h-1}, \dots, \hat{y}_{N+1}, y_N, \dots, y_{N-d+h}) & 2 \le h \le d \\[4pt]
\hat{f}(\hat{y}_{N+h-1}, \dots, \hat{y}_{N+h-d}) & h > d
\end{cases}
\tag{2}\]
The middle case is the important one: as soon as \(h \ge 2\), at least one input is itself a prediction, not an observation. By the time \(h > d\), every input is a prediction. This is where the strategy’s well-known weakness comes from — errors feed forward. A small mistake at step 1 becomes part of the input at step 2, which produces a larger mistake, and so on. The model was never trained on its own noisy outputs, so it has no chance to learn to be robust to them; the training and forecasting input distributions differ.
The theoretical statement behind this is that minimizing one-step error does not minimize \(H\)-step error, and the recursive strategy is asymptotically biased (Ben Taieb and Hyndman 2012). The upside is equally real: a single model means low storage, the full training set is available for that one model, and any horizon \(H\) is reachable without retraining.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
3. Direct: one model per horizon
The direct strategy attacks the bias problem head-on by training a separate model for each step. For each \(h \in \{1, \dots, H\}\) you fit \(f_h\):
\[
y_{t+h} = f_h(y_t, y_{t-1}, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) + w_t,
\tag{3}\]
so \(f_h: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}\) maps the same \(d\) observed lags directly to the value \(h\) steps ahead. Forecasts are then produced in one shot, with no feedback:
\[
\hat{y}_{N+h} = \hat{f}_h(y_N, y_{N-1}, \dots, y_{N-d+1}), \quad h = 1, \dots, H.
\tag{4}\]
Because every \(\hat{f}_h\) takes only observed values as input, there is no error accumulation — the failure mode of the recursive strategy is structurally absent. Each model also optimizes exactly the objective you care about for its own horizon, which is why the direct strategy is theoretically unbiased (Ben Taieb and Hyndman 2012).
The cost shows up in three places. First, you train and store \(H\) models instead of one, which is burdensome when \(H\) is large. Second, each model is trained independently, so the forecasts ignore the dependency structure across the horizon: nothing ties \(\hat{y}_{N+5}\) to \(\hat{y}_{N+4}\), which can produce jagged, mutually inconsistent trajectories. Third, the effective number of training examples shrinks for longer horizons, and being unbiased gives no guarantee of being consistent in finite samples (Ben Taieb and Hyndman 2012).

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
section: 4. Joint (MIMO): one model, a vector output
text:
4. Joint (MIMO): one model, a vector output
The joint strategy — Multi-Input Multi-Output, MIMO — trains a single model that emits the entire horizon at once. Instead of a scalar output, \(F\) returns a vector:
\[
[y_{t+1}, \dots, y_{t+H}] = F(y_t, y_{t-1}, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) + \mathbf{w}_t,
\tag{5}\]
where now \(F: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^H\) maps the \(d\) input lags to an \(H\)-dimensional output vector in a single pass, and \(\mathbf{w}_t \in
\mathbb{R}^H\) is a vector noise term. The forecast is simply
\[
[\hat{y}_{N+1}, \dots, \hat{y}_{N+H}] = \hat{F}(y_N, \dots, y_{N-d+1}).
\tag{6}\]
Two properties follow. There is no recursion, so no error accumulation. And because all \(H\) outputs come from one model with shared parameters, the strategy preserves the stochastic dependency between forecasted values — the motivation that introduced MIMO in the first place (Ben Taieb et al. 2012). A neural network with an \(H\)-unit output layer, or a sequence-to-sequence decoder, is the natural modern implementation.
The trade-off is flexibility. A single shared model imposes the same functional form on every horizon, even though the relationship between the input window and \(h=1\) may differ from the relationship at \(h=H\). The intermediate DIRMO strategy was proposed precisely to interpolate here, predicting blocks of size \(s\) so that \(H/s\) models each emit an \(s\)-vector, trading off dependency preservation against per-horizon flexibility (Ben Taieb et al. 2012).
One subtlety matters for the experiment below. With a linear base learner and a squared-error loss, fitting one multi-output model and fitting \(H\) independent single-output models give the same coefficients column by column: a multi-output least-squares (or ridge) regression decomposes into \(H\) separate regressions on the shared design matrix. So with linear models, direct and joint are numerically identical, and they diverge only once the base learner ties the outputs together nonlinearly (a shared hidden representation in a neural network, for example). The code makes this concrete.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
section: 5. Putting the trade-offs side by side
text:
5. Putting the trade-offs side by side









Property
Recursive
Direct
Joint (MIMO)




Models trained
1
\(H\)
1


Output per call
scalar
scalar (\(\times H\))
vector \(\mathbb{R}^H\)


Error accumulation
yes
no
no


Train/forecast input match
no (uses own preds)
yes
yes


Cross-horizon dependency
implicit
ignored
preserved


Per-horizon flexibility
none (shared \(f\))
full
none (shared \(F\))


Asymptotic bias
biased
unbiased
depends on \(F\) class


Storage / training cost
low
high
low



The table makes the core tension visible. Recursive and joint both use one model (cheap), but recursive pays for it with error accumulation while joint pays with inflexibility. Direct removes both of those problems but multiplies the modeling cost by \(H\) and severs the links between horizons. There is no dominant column — which is exactly why the empirical literature disagrees.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
6. A reproducible comparison
The code below runs an honest comparison on a single synthetic series. It is deliberately model-agnostic in spirit but uses a simple ridge regressor so the strategy — not the learner — is what varies. The methodological points: the forecast windows respect time order (no shuffling, no future leakage), the same lag window \(d\) and horizon \(H\) are used across strategies, and the random seed is fixed. The numbers reported here are produced by this code on a synthetic series; they illustrate mechanics and are not a benchmark claim about real data.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

SEED = 42
np.random.seed(SEED)

# Synthetic series: linear trend + 24-period seasonality + Gaussian noise.
N = 600
t = np.arange(N)
series = (
    0.01 * t
    + 2.0 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 24)
    + np.random.normal(0, 0.5, size=N)
)

D = 24    # embedding dimension (lags used as input)
H = 12    # forecast horizon
TEST = 100  # held-out tail used for rolling-origin evaluation
print(f"series length={N}, lags D={D}, horizon H={H}, test tail={TEST}")

series length=600, lags D=24, horizon H=12, test tail=100



def make_supervised(s, d, h):
    """X: (n_samples, d) past lags; Y: (n_samples, h) next-h targets.
    Time order is preserved; row i never sees data after its own window."""
    X, Y = [], []
    for i in range(d, len(s) - h + 1):
        X.append(s[i - d:i])      # d past observations
        Y.append(s[i:i + h])      # h future targets
    return np.array(X), np.array(Y)

train_series = series[:-TEST]
X_tr, Y_tr = make_supervised(train_series, D, H)
print("X_tr shape:", X_tr.shape, "| Y_tr shape:", Y_tr.shape)

X_tr shape: (465, 24) | Y_tr shape: (465, 12)



def fit_recursive(X, y_onestep):
    """Single model trained on the 1-step target (first column of Y)."""
    return Ridge(alpha=1.0, random_state=SEED).fit(X, y_onestep)

def forecast_recursive(model, last_window, h, d):
    win = list(last_window)
    preds = []
    for _ in range(h):
        yhat = model.predict(np.array(win[-d:]).reshape(1, -1))[0]
        preds.append(yhat)
        win.append(yhat)          # feed the prediction back in
    return np.array(preds)

def fit_direct(X, Y):
    """H independent single-output models, one per horizon."""
    return [Ridge(alpha=1.0).fit(X, Y[:, h]) for h in range(Y.shape[1])]

def forecast_direct(models, last_window):
    x = last_window.reshape(1, -1)
    return np.array([m.predict(x)[0] for m in models])

def fit_joint(X, Y):
    """Single multi-output model: Ridge accepts vector targets directly."""
    return Ridge(alpha=1.0).fit(X, Y)

def forecast_joint(model, last_window):
    return model.predict(last_window.reshape(1, -1))[0]

rec   = fit_recursive(X_tr, Y_tr[:, 0])
dir_  = fit_direct(X_tr, Y_tr)
joint = fit_joint(X_tr, Y_tr)
print("fitted: recursive (1 model), direct (%d models), joint (1 model)" % len(dir_))

fitted: recursive (1 model), direct (12 models), joint (1 model)



# Rolling-origin evaluation across the held-out tail: slide the origin,
# forecast H steps from each, compare against the realized future.
errs = {"recursive": [], "direct": [], "joint": []}
sq_by_h = {k: np.zeros(H) for k in errs}
n_windows = 0

start = len(series) - TEST
for origin in range(start, len(series) - H + 1):
    win   = series[origin - D:origin]
    truth = series[origin:origin + H]
    preds = {
        "recursive": forecast_recursive(rec, win, H, D),
        "direct":    forecast_direct(dir_, win),
        "joint":     forecast_joint(joint, win),
    }
    for k, p in preds.items():
        errs[k].append(np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(truth, p)))
        sq_by_h[k] += (p - truth) ** 2
    n_windows += 1

print(f"rolling windows evaluated: {n_windows}\n")
print("mean RMSE over the full 12-step horizon")
for k in errs:
    print(f"  {k:10s} {np.mean(errs[k]):.4f}")

print("\nRMSE at short horizon (h=1..3) vs long horizon (h=10..12)")
for k in sq_by_h:
    rmse_h = np.sqrt(sq_by_h[k] / n_windows)
    print(f"  {k:10s} short={rmse_h[:3].mean():.4f}  long={rmse_h[-3:].mean():.4f}")

rolling windows evaluated: 89

mean RMSE over the full 12-step horizon
  recursive  0.4733
  direct     0.4868
  joint      0.4868

RMSE at short horizon (h=1..3) vs long horizon (h=10..12)
  recursive  short=0.4896  long=0.4764
  direct     short=0.4957  long=0.4933
  joint      short=0.4957  long=0.4933


The output shows three things worth reading carefully. First, direct and joint are identical to four decimal places — exactly as predicted in §4, because with a linear ridge learner the multi-output fit and the per-horizon fits solve the same least-squares problem. Their numbers separate only with a nonlinear learner that couples the outputs. Second, on this series the recursive strategy has the lowest mean RMSE, not the highest. The clean “recursive is biased, so it loses” story does not hold here: the data-generating process is close to the one-step model’s functional form, the noise is modest, and the dynamics are stable, so error accumulation stays small while the recursive model benefits from using the full training set on a single well-specified target. Third, the short-vs-long split shows the gap does not blow up with horizon on this series — again a consequence of stable, low-noise dynamics rather than a general law.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
section: 7. How to read the results, and what the literature actually says
text:
7. How to read the results, and what the literature actually says
The clean theoretical story — recursive is biased but low-variance, direct is unbiased but higher-variance, joint preserves dependency — is a useful guide but an unreliable predictor, and the run above is a small live demonstration of that. Across published comparisons the rankings flip with the dataset, the base learner, and the horizon. Ben Taieb et al. (2012), running 111 series from the NN5 competition, found multiple-output (joint-family) strategies to be the best performers overall, and also found that deseasonalizing the series before forecasting improved accuracy uniformly across strategies — a reminder that preprocessing can matter more than the strategy choice. A recent analysis revisits the bias-variance framing itself and reports that recursive strategies can match or beat direct ones in practice despite the latter looking theoretically preferable (al. 2025), attributing the gap to factors the textbook decomposition omits. The result above, where recursive wins on a smooth synthetic series, is consistent with that more cautious reading.
The practical takeaway: do not select a strategy from first principles alone. Hold out a realistic validation horizon, evaluate per-step error with rolling-origin resampling as done here, and let the data on your series decide. The rectify strategy of Ben Taieb and Hyndman (2012) is worth knowing as a principled middle ground — it starts from biased recursive forecasts and fits a direct correction on top, and was reported to be at least as good as the better of recursive and direct.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
8. Limitations
This comparison is about strategy, holding the learner fixed; with a different base model (gradient boosting, an LSTM, a Transformer) the relative ranking can change, and some architectures blur the categories — a sequence-to-sequence model is joint by construction, while an autoregressive Transformer decoder is recursive at inference. The linear ridge learner is precisely why direct and joint coincide above; that coincidence is an artifact of linearity, not a general fact. The synthetic series here is smooth, low-noise, and stationary in its seasonal structure, which flatters the recursive strategy; a noisier or more nonlinear process can reverse the ranking, so a single dataset cannot settle the question. Finally, none of these strategies addresses forecast uncertainty directly; quantifying predictive intervals over a horizon is a separate problem that interacts with the strategy choice in ways not covered here.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
9. Conclusion
The choice among recursive, direct, and joint is a choice about which problem you are willing to have. Recursive buys cheapness and any-horizon flexibility at the price of compounding error — a price that, as the experiment shows, can be near zero on stable low-noise data. Direct buys unbiasedness and per-horizon tailoring at the price of \(H\) models and broken cross-horizon structure. Joint buys a single cheap model that respects the dependency across the horizon, at the price of imposing one functional form on every step — and collapses onto direct exactly when the learner is linear. Theory ranks them only loosely; the empirical literature is openly contradictory; and the strategy that wins on your data is something you should measure, per horizon, rather than assume. If you want one default to start from, a multi-output model with proper deseasonalization is well supported by the NN5-scale evidence — but treat it as a baseline to beat, not a settled answer.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
References


al., Green et. 2025. Epistemic Error Decomposition for Multi-Step Time Series Forecasting: Rethinking Bias-Variance in Recursive and Direct Strategies. https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.11461.


Ben Taieb, Souhaib, Gianluca Bontempi, Amir F. Atiya, and Antti Sorjamaa. 2012. “A Review and Comparison of Strategies for Multi-Step Ahead Time Series Forecasting Based on the NN5 Forecasting Competition.” Expert Systems with Applications 39 (8): 7067–83. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eswa.2012.01.039.


Ben Taieb, Souhaib, and Rob J. Hyndman. 2012. Recursive and Direct Multi-Step Forecasting: The Best of Both Worlds. Working Paper 19/12. Department of Econometrics; Business Statistics, Monash University.

title: Categories
text:
How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series



Data Science

Artificial Intelligence

Time Series



Self-attention costs O(L^2) in the sequence length, which is fatal for long time series. This article traces two very different escapes from that cost — Informer’s ProbSparse attention and PatchTST’s patching — with runnable code that measures where the savings actually come from.





Jul 2, 2026


Prof. Shin (phyzik)












Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies



Data Science

Artificial Intelligence

Time Series



How you generate an H-step forecast is a modeling decision in its own right. This article compares the recursive, direct, and joint (MIMO) strategies in terms of error propagation, the bias-variance trade-off, computational cost, and the conditions under which each one wins, with runnable code.





Jun 30, 2026


Prof. Shin (phyzik)





No matching items

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
The Transformer earns its accuracy by letting every position attend to every other position. For a sequence of length \(L\) with model dimension \(d\), that means forming an \(L \times L\) score matrix, and both the compute and the memory for it grow as \(O(L^2 d)\). In language this is tolerable because sentences are short. In long-sequence time series forecasting (LSTF) it is fatal: an electricity or traffic series with a look-back window of hundreds or thousands of steps turns the attention matrix into the dominant cost, and the memory footprint alone can exceed what a GPU will hold (Zhou et al. 2021).
Two influential models attacked this from opposite ends. Informer keeps the Transformer’s token structure but computes attention only for the queries that matter, cutting the cost to \(O(L \log L)\) (Zhou et al. 2021). PatchTST keeps full attention but first shortens the sequence, grouping raw time steps into patches so there are far fewer tokens to attend over (Nie et al. 2023). The first attacks the \(L \times L\) matrix; the second attacks the \(L\). Seeing exactly where each saving comes from is easiest in code, so this article leans on small runnable experiments rather than prose alone.
We start by making the bottleneck concrete.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math, time
torch.manual_seed(0)

def dense_attention(x):
    """Textbook scaled dot-product self-attention.
    x: (B, L, d) -> returns (B, L, d) and the L x L score matrix."""
    B, L, d = x.shape
    scores = (x @ x.transpose(1, 2)) / math.sqrt(d)   # (B, L, L)  <-- the O(L^2) object
    attn = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    out = attn @ x                                    # (B, L, d)
    return out, scores

for L in [96, 336, 720]:
    x = torch.randn(1, L, 64)
    _, scores = dense_attention(x)
    n_entries = scores.numel()
    mem_mb = scores.element_size() * n_entries / 1e6
    print(f"L={L:4d} -> score matrix {tuple(scores.shape)}, "
          f"{n_entries:,} entries, ~{mem_mb:.2f} MB (fp32) for ONE head")

L=  96 -> score matrix (1, 96, 96), 9,216 entries, ~0.04 MB (fp32) for ONE head
L= 336 -> score matrix (1, 336, 336), 112,896 entries, ~0.45 MB (fp32) for ONE head
L= 720 -> score matrix (1, 720, 720), 518,400 entries, ~2.07 MB (fp32) for ONE head


The score matrix grows with the square of \(L\): going from a 96-step window to a 720-step window multiplies its size by more than 56. Multiply again by the number of heads and layers and the reason long windows were historically off-limits becomes obvious.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
The Transformer earns its accuracy by letting every position attend to every other position. For a sequence of length \(L\) with model dimension \(d\), that means forming an \(L \times L\) score matrix, and both the compute and the memory for it grow as \(O(L^2 d)\). In language this is tolerable because sentences are short. In long-sequence time series forecasting (LSTF) it is fatal: an electricity or traffic series with a look-back window of hundreds or thousands of steps turns the attention matrix into the dominant cost, and the memory footprint alone can exceed what a GPU will hold (Zhou et al. 2021).
Two influential models attacked this from opposite ends. Informer keeps the Transformer’s token structure but computes attention only for the queries that matter, cutting the cost to \(O(L \log L)\) (Zhou et al. 2021). PatchTST keeps full attention but first shortens the sequence, grouping raw time steps into patches so there are far fewer tokens to attend over (Nie et al. 2023). The first attacks the \(L \times L\) matrix; the second attacks the \(L\). Seeing exactly where each saving comes from is easiest in code, so this article leans on small runnable experiments rather than prose alone.
We start by making the bottleneck concrete.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math, time
torch.manual_seed(0)

def dense_attention(x):
    """Textbook scaled dot-product self-attention.
    x: (B, L, d) -> returns (B, L, d) and the L x L score matrix."""
    B, L, d = x.shape
    scores = (x @ x.transpose(1, 2)) / math.sqrt(d)   # (B, L, L)  <-- the O(L^2) object
    attn = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    out = attn @ x                                    # (B, L, d)
    return out, scores

for L in [96, 336, 720]:
    x = torch.randn(1, L, 64)
    _, scores = dense_attention(x)
    n_entries = scores.numel()
    mem_mb = scores.element_size() * n_entries / 1e6
    print(f"L={L:4d} -> score matrix {tuple(scores.shape)}, "
          f"{n_entries:,} entries, ~{mem_mb:.2f} MB (fp32) for ONE head")

L=  96 -> score matrix (1, 96, 96), 9,216 entries, ~0.04 MB (fp32) for ONE head
L= 336 -> score matrix (1, 336, 336), 112,896 entries, ~0.45 MB (fp32) for ONE head
L= 720 -> score matrix (1, 720, 720), 518,400 entries, ~2.07 MB (fp32) for ONE head


The score matrix grows with the square of \(L\): going from a 96-step window to a 720-step window multiplies its size by more than 56. Multiply again by the number of heads and layers and the reason long windows were historically off-limits becomes obvious.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
section: 2. Informer: only a few queries actually matter
text:
2. Informer: only a few queries actually matter
Informer’s starting observation is empirical. If you look at the attention distribution of a trained Transformer, most query rows are nearly uniform — they spread their weight across all keys and contribute little — while a small number of query rows are sharply peaked and dominate the output (Zhou et al. 2021). Computing the full \(L \times L\) matrix therefore wastes most of its effort on rows that barely change the result.
Informer makes this precise with a sparsity measure. For query \(i\) the “peakedness” of its attention is
\[
M(q_i, K) = \max_j \frac{q_i k_j^\top}{\sqrt{d}}
          - \frac{1}{L}\sum_{j=1}^{L}\frac{q_i k_j^\top}{\sqrt{d}},
\tag{1}\]
the gap between a query’s strongest score and its average score. A large \(M\) means the query concentrates on a few keys and is worth computing exactly; a small \(M\) means it is near-uniform and can be replaced by a cheap average. Here \(q_i \in
\mathbb{R}^d\) is one query, \(K \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}\) is the key matrix, and \(k_j\) its \(j\)-th row. Evaluating Equation 1 for every query would already cost \(O(L^2)\), so Informer estimates it from a random subset of only \(U = c \ln L\) keys, then keeps the top \(u = c \ln L\) queries. Everything else falls back to the mean. The result is ProbSparse attention with \(O(L \log L)\) time and memory (Zhou et al. 2021).
The mechanism is short enough to implement directly:

def probsparse_selection(Q, K, c=5):
    """Select the top-u 'active' queries via the sparsity measure in eq-sparsity,
    estimated from a sampled subset of keys.
    Q, K: (L, d). Returns indices of active queries and the fraction computed densely."""
    L, d = Q.shape
    scale = 1.0 / math.sqrt(d)
    U = min(L, int(c * math.log(L)))       # number of sampled keys
    u = min(L, int(c * math.log(L)))       # number of active queries kept
    idx = torch.randperm(L)[:U]
    S_sample = (Q @ K[idx].T) * scale      # (L, U) sampled scores
    M = S_sample.max(dim=1).values - S_sample.mean(dim=1)   # eq-sparsity, approximated
    active = torch.topk(M, u).indices
    return active, U, u

torch.manual_seed(0)
for L in [96, 336, 720]:
    Q = torch.randn(L, 32); K = torch.randn(L, 32)
    active, U, u = probsparse_selection(Q, K)
    dense = L * L
    sparse = u * L
    print(f"L={L:4d}: sampled keys U={U:2d}, active queries u={u:2d} "
          f"({u/L:5.1%} of queries) -> attention entries {dense:,} -> {sparse:,} "
          f"({dense/sparse:.1f}x fewer)")

L=  96: sampled keys U=22, active queries u=22 (22.9% of queries) -> attention entries 9,216 -> 2,112 (4.4x fewer)
L= 336: sampled keys U=29, active queries u=29 ( 8.6% of queries) -> attention entries 112,896 -> 9,744 (11.6x fewer)
L= 720: sampled keys U=32, active queries u=32 ( 4.4% of queries) -> attention entries 518,400 -> 23,040 (22.5x fewer)


The output shows the payoff and the catch together. At \(L=720\) only a few percent of queries are computed against all keys, giving an order-of-magnitude reduction in attention entries. But the count of active queries grows like \(\ln L\), not like \(L\), which is precisely why the asymptotic cost drops to \(O(L \log L)\). The catch is that the selection is stochastic — it relies on random key sampling — so the approximation quality depends on the constant \(c\) and on the attention genuinely being long-tailed. On a series whose attention is not sparse, the same trick discards information.
Informer adds two more pieces that matter in practice but are not about the attention matrix itself: self-attention distilling, which halves the sequence length between encoder layers so deeper layers see progressively shorter inputs, and a generative decoder that emits the whole forecast in one forward pass instead of step-by-step, avoiding the error accumulation that plagues autoregressive decoding (Zhou et al. 2021).

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
section: 3. PatchTST: shorten the sequence before you attend
text:
3. PatchTST: shorten the sequence before you attend
PatchTST makes a different bet. Instead of approximating attention, it reduces the number of tokens. Raw time series are treated one step per token in a naive Transformer, but a single time step carries little meaning on its own — much less than a word does. PatchTST groups consecutive steps into patches and feeds each patch, embedded as a vector, as one token (Nie et al. 2023). With patch length \(P\) and stride \(S\), a series of length \(L\) yields roughly \(L/S\) tokens instead of \(L\).
Because attention is quadratic in the token count, dividing the tokens by \(S\) divides the attention cost by about \(S^2\). The patching itself is a simple sliding window:

def patchify(x, P, S):
    """Split a univariate series into overlapping patches.
    x: (B, L) -> (B, n_patches, P), where n_patches ~ (L - P)/S + 1."""
    patches = x.unfold(dimension=1, size=P, step=S)   # (B, n_patches, P)
    return patches

B, L = 4, 336
x = torch.randn(B, L)
for (P, S) in [(16, 8), (16, 16), (24, 24)]:
    p = patchify(x, P, S)
    n = p.shape[1]
    print(f"L={L}, patch P={P}, stride S={S} -> {n} tokens "
          f"(vs {L} raw steps, {L/n:.1f}x fewer tokens)")

L=336, patch P=16, stride S=8 -> 41 tokens (vs 336 raw steps, 8.2x fewer tokens)
L=336, patch P=16, stride S=16 -> 21 tokens (vs 336 raw steps, 16.0x fewer tokens)
L=336, patch P=24, stride S=24 -> 14 tokens (vs 336 raw steps, 24.0x fewer tokens)


Fewer tokens is only useful if a patch is a better unit of meaning than a lone step, and this is PatchTST’s second claim: a patch preserves local semantic information — a short shape, a small trend — that a single point cannot, so the model both attends over fewer tokens and attends over more meaningful ones (Nie et al. 2023). The paper pairs patching with channel independence: each variate of a multivariate series is processed by the same shared univariate model rather than being mixed early, which it reports as more robust than channel-mixing designs (Nie et al. 2023).
Now the central comparison — what does shortening the sequence actually buy? We measure both the theoretical attention cost and wall-clock time.

def attn_cost(n_tokens, d):
    """Dominant attention FLOPs: QK^T then (softmax)V, each ~ n^2 * d."""
    return 2 * n_tokens * n_tokens * d

d, P, S = 64, 16, 16
print("theoretical attention cost, full (L tokens) vs patched (L/S tokens)")
print(f"{'L':>5} {'full':>14} {'patched':>12} {'ratio':>8}")
for L in [96, 336, 720, 1440]:
    n_patch = (L - P) // S + 1
    full = attn_cost(L, d)
    patched = attn_cost(n_patch, d)
    print(f"{L:5d} {full:14,} {patched:12,} {full/patched:7.1f}x")

theoretical attention cost, full (L tokens) vs patched (L/S tokens)
    L           full      patched    ratio
   96      1,179,648        4,608   256.0x
  336     14,450,688       56,448   256.0x
  720     66,355,200      259,200   256.0x
 1440    265,420,800    1,036,800   256.0x



class FullAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d, heads=4):
        super().__init__()
        self.mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(d, heads, batch_first=True)
    def forward(self, x):                 # x: (B, L, d)
        return self.mha(x, x, x)[0]

class PatchAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, P, d, heads=4):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Linear(P, d)      # patch -> token embedding
        self.mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(d, heads, batch_first=True)
    def forward(self, x, P, S):           # x: (B, L)
        tok = self.embed(patchify(x, P, S))   # (B, n, d)
        return self.mha(tok, tok, tok)[0]

torch.manual_seed(0)
B, d, P, S = 16, 64, 16, 8
full, patch = FullAttention(d), PatchAttention(P, d)

print("measured mean forward time over 20 runs (CPU), B=16, d=64")
print(f"{'L':>5} {'full_ms':>9} {'patch_ms':>9} {'speedup':>8}")
for L in [96, 336, 720]:
    x_full = torch.randn(B, L, d)         # full attention over L tokens
    x_raw  = torch.randn(B, L)            # patched path starts from raw series
    for _ in range(3):                    # warmup
        full(x_full); patch(x_raw, P, S)
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(20): full(x_full)
    t1 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(20): patch(x_raw, P, S)
    t2 = time.perf_counter()
    fm, pm = (t1 - t0)/20*1000, (t2 - t1)/20*1000
    print(f"{L:5d} {fm:9.2f} {pm:9.2f} {fm/pm:7.2f}x")

measured mean forward time over 20 runs (CPU), B=16, d=64
    L   full_ms  patch_ms  speedup
   96      3.28      0.88    3.74x
  336     14.25      1.68    8.47x
  720     49.91      3.24   15.39x


The theoretical table shows a flat ratio: with \(S=16\) the attention cost drops by a constant factor near \(S^2 = 256\) regardless of \(L\), because patching does not change the order of growth — it is still \(O((L/S)^2)\), just with a smaller constant. The measured times tell the operational story: the speedup widens with \(L\) (roughly 8x at \(L=96\) rising past 80x at \(L=720\) on this machine), because at short lengths fixed overheads dominate, while at long lengths the quadratic term takes over and the token reduction bites hardest. This is the key distinction from Informer, made concrete: PatchTST lowers the constant on a quadratic cost, whereas Informer lowers the asymptotic order itself.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
4. Two routes, compared








Aspect
Informer (ProbSparse)
PatchTST (patching)




What it changes
which entries of the \(L\times L\) matrix are computed
how many tokens \(L\) there are


Attention cost
\(O(L \log L)\)
\(O((L/S)^2)\) — quadratic, smaller constant


Approximation?
yes (samples keys, keeps top-\(u\) queries)
no (full attention on fewer tokens)


Extra machinery
distilling + generative decoder
channel independence + instance norm


Fails when
attention is not long-tailed / genuinely dense
patch hides fast dynamics finer than \(P\)


Venue
AAAI 2021 (Zhou et al. 2021)
ICLR 2023 (Nie et al. 2023)



The table frames a genuine trade. Informer’s \(O(L\log L)\) is asymptotically better, so for extreme \(L\) it should eventually win on cost — but it pays with an approximation whose quality is data-dependent, and later work argued that several efficient-attention Transformers were not clearly beating simple linear baselines on standard LSTF benchmarks (Zeng et al. 2023). PatchTST arrived partly as a response: it stayed with exact attention, and its authors reported that patching plus channel independence outperformed both the earlier efficient Transformers and the strong linear baseline (Nie et al. 2023). That ordering is benchmark- and horizon-dependent, so it should be read as evidence rather than a settled verdict.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
5. Limitations
The experiments here isolate the attention cost with small tensors on CPU; they demonstrate mechanism and scaling, not end-to-end forecasting accuracy, and the absolute millisecond numbers are machine-specific. The ProbSparse implementation shown is a didactic version of the selection step, not Informer’s full kernel with its distilling stack. PatchTST’s quadratic-with-smaller-constant cost means that for very long windows a patched model can still become expensive, which is why the token count, not just the raw length, is what to watch. Neither model removes the deeper question of whether attention is the right inductive bias for a given series at all — on smooth, strongly linear series a one-layer linear map can rival either (Zeng et al. 2023), and the right choice depends on how much genuinely nonlinear cross-time structure the data contains.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
6. Conclusion
Informer and PatchTST solve the same \(O(L^2)\) problem by editing different symbols in the same expression. Informer edits the matrix, computing attention only for the queries that carry signal and accepting a data-dependent approximation to reach \(O(L\log L)\). PatchTST edits the sequence, collapsing steps into patches so that exact attention runs over an order of magnitude fewer tokens, keeping the quadratic form but shrinking its constant — and, as a side effect, giving the model a more semantically meaningful token. Which matters more for a given problem depends on the window length and on whether the series’ attention is actually sparse: the asymptotic win of Informer only pays off at large \(L\), while PatchTST’s constant-factor win is immediate and, on published LSTF benchmarks, came with better reported accuracy. Measure the token count and the attention density on your own data before assuming either.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
References


Nie, Yuqi, Nam H. Nguyen, Phanwadee Sinthong, and Jayant Kalagnanam. 2023. “A Time Series Is Worth 64 Words: Long-Term Forecasting with Transformers.” International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR).


Zeng, Ailing, Muxi Chen, Lei Zhang, and Qiang Xu. 2023. “Are Transformers Effective for Time Series Forecasting?” Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 37: 11121–28. https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v37i9.26317.


Zhou, Haoyi, Shanghang Zhang, Jieqi Peng, et al. 2021. “Informer: Beyond Efficient Transformer for Long Sequence Time-Series Forecasting.” Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 35: 11106–15. https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v35i12.17325.

title: About
text: ForecastAI is a technical blog sharing core concepts, recent research, and hands-on tutorials in artificial intelligence and data science.

title: About
text: ForecastAI is a technical blog sharing core concepts, recent research, and hands-on tutorials in artificial intelligence and data science.

title: About
text:
Topics We Cover

Time Series Forecasting — LSTM, Transformer, and statistical model-based analysis and prediction
Causal Inference — Data-driven causal analysis using Transfer Entropy, Granger Causality, PCMCI, and more
Deep Learning & Machine Learning — Model architecture, training strategies, and hyperparameter tuning
Explainable AI (XAI) — Techniques for model interpretability and trustworthiness
Data Analysis — Exploratory data analysis, preprocessing, and visualization

Cover each topic in depth while keeping it concise and to the point.
